replies.author: @2012sonora
replies.text:
​@mlaprarie As a service academy grad, the comm badge conversation is something that could and would happen with a freshman cadet. God, the stupid things that happen in that place... for example, we used to have something called "carrier landings" where the whole squadron would lube up a section of hallway with liquid laundry soap, get a mattress, and take turns throwing the littlest person in the squadron onto the mattress as hard as possible and seeing how far down the hallway the mattress would glide. That was ended when somebody broke their jaw one night. Or sputniks, where you'd get an apple at lunch, stick a fork in it, then toss it to the cadet next to you, who had to catch it on their fork and toss in on, usually going until somebody got hurt. Or the spring when we had a vole invasion in the dorms and people started trapping and hanging the dead things up on their doors as "a warning to the others." 

Future fighter pilots right there, y'all. Actually a lot of stuff we did involved injury somehow. 

But it's a weird mix of extreme intelligence, boredom, and the stress of the environment that does it, not innate idiocy. Usually.

author: @RedBeard1682
text:
After dealing with 30 minutes of Academy, I had to put an episode of DS9 on to clear my palate.  It was 'The Wire'.  It was Garak dealing with his depression of exile through drugs, and Dr. Bashir trying to help him in the best way possible.  It invited the audience to confront the issues of political exile/post-war consequences, drug use, suicide and friendship.  It was real in the sense that you as an audience member could relate, and it brought up a moral conversation.  This was a classic Roddenberry style episode.  We confronted societal issues through the lens of a science fiction show.  Roddenberry let guys in bad alien costumes bring forth troubling issues so we could deal it without having to face our ugly reality.  

With Kurtzman, we encounter issues, but he has removed the discussion. We are told what is right and what is wrong, and the moral conclusion is preloaded into the episode before the audience is allowed to think. As you said in the video, it is now a show “by idiots and for idiots” because an idiot cannot be allowed to be questioned. A thinking audience might compromise the message, and by removing the discussion, he has removed the soul of Star Trek. They have taken the 45-minute morality story and turned it into serialized propaganda with lens flares.

replies.author: @psilynt1
replies.text: This is exactly how I feel about the problems of modern trek. Especially Academy. Gone are the days of allegory and using storytelling to invite contemplation on a perspective. It's just dropping the issue into a character, showing it as accepted and skipping the storytelling in favor of action or unrelated drama.

replies.author: @lomaxrobert
replies.text: This is a broader problem with movies and TV now. The character's morality, motivations and 'right' choices must be transparent and spoonfed to the audience. Nothing can be nuanced or uncertain or unresolved. There is only one way to see the movie, and only one correct way to feel about what you have seen. Not only that but the actors must reflect or at least not create conflict with that messaging in their private lives too. In the New Yorker, Namwali Serpell calls this the New Literalism. “When I say literalism, I don’t mean realistic or plainly literal,” she writes. “I mean literalist, as when we say something is on the nose or heavy-handed, that it hammers away at us or beats a dead horse. As these phrases imply, to re-state the screamingly obvious does a kind of violence to art.”

author: @fidel-3470
text: It's worth keeping in mind that a lot of what passes as "journalism" is what we in the industry called "paid media engagement." i.e., someone is paying them to write an article, often telling them what to write. And it's particularly sinister because the connections are not always clear: the compensation for writing these articles is buried deep in advertising budgets of parent corporations. The parent company of the newspaper works with the parent company of the studio and they form the media relationship across multiple different properties, not simply this one show/film, but everything the studio puts out.

replies.text:
As the parent of an autistic son, I agree 1000%. My son is not "neurodivergent." He's autistic. He has a clinical diagnosis of autism, which puts him on the autistic spectrum. That makes him no more and no less special than anyone else. It just makes him different - just like everyone else is different from every other person. Whenever we pull these moronic labels like "neurodivergent" or "neurotypical," we just find new ways to isolate, stereotype and put ourselves into little boxes. 

Screw them and their ignorant words.

replies.author: @johntheknight3062
replies.text: @TheDoctor1225 It is so refreshing to see someone with actual issue acknowledge this. As you say, it is more damaging to people like your son than anyone esle. Every random idiot can hide behind these labels, pretending to be special. I saw just too many of these instances. I am so glad for people like you.

author: @Jungle1000
text: I was watching Star Trek before Rachel Leishman was born. Nobody gets to tell what is ours and what is not. Star Trek was never about forcing a single viewpoint down anyones throat. It was about presenting us with a puzzle or controversy, experiencing the crews attempts and strategies to overcome it. And most importantly there was always a moment of reflection and rationalization at the end. We felt the rawness of the decision making and the often times emotional toll. It allowed us to grow from these processes organically. No better example exists than DS9 "In The Pale Moonlight".  Sisko worked with former Cardassian spy Garak to bomb a Romulan vessel and implicate the Dominion, with the Romulans declaring war on the Dominion as a result. The conflict and ultimately justification that took place are a masterclass of what is missing with todays Trek.
